coredata.dc:title: High-efficient and pH-sensitive orange luminescence from silicon-doped carbon dots for information encryption and bio-imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zishan
Zhou, Weiying
Luo, Jiabao
Fan, Jiaqi
Wu, Zhan-chao
Zhu, Hongni
Huang, Jinqing
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: The exploration of carbon dots (CDs) with high quantum yield, facile synthesis path and satisfying output for their multiple applications remains a challenge. Thus, a silicon-doped orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) is synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method o-phenylenediamine and ethyl orthosilicate as raw materials. The O-CDs exhibits a bright and non-excitation-dependent emission peaking at 580 nm, and the corresponding quantum yield could be greatly boosted from 39.2 % to 64.1 % by silicon doping. The obtained O-CDs possess good biocompatibility and promising luminescence stability with varying solvents, ionic concentrations and temperatures. Its bio-imaging ability is performed by incubating zebrafish embryos with O-CDs aqueous solution, and clear in-vivo fluorescent images are obtained. Furthermore, due to its high-efficient and specific pH-sensitive emission with excellent dispersibility, the O-CDs can be used as a fluorescent ink for dual-model data encry/decryption in both hand-writing and stamp printing. Therefore, the as-prepared O-CDs show the potential as promising candidate for biomedical diagnosis, data encryption, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Doping
Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting
Bio-imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image of O-CDs (a) and the inset illustration shows the particle size distribution. The FT-IR spectra (b) and the wide scan XPS spectrum of O-CDs (c), high resolution XPS of C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), O 1 s (f) and Si 2p3 (g) spectra of O-CDs (h).

Excitation and emission spectra (a) and PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths (b) and in different solvents (c), ionic strengths (d) and temperatures (e) at 410 nm excitation, and the time-resolved decay lifetime (f) of the O-CDs.
Fluorescence images and bright field images are used to observe zebrafish embryos exposed to different concentrations of O-CDs.

PL spectra of O-CDs in various pH conditions at 410 nm excitation, error bar was obtained by repeating three measurements (a) and the decay variation of the O-CDs with acidic and alkaline treatment (b). Photographs of the poem which writing by O-CDs inks under sunlight and UV light, and images when the pome is encrypted and decrypted (c).
Schematic illustration of information encryption and decryption processes of the O-CDs stamp printing.
The starch power and hydrogel of O-CDs under sunlight (a), and under 395 nm UV lamp (b), and the PL spectra of the O-CDs@ starch (c) and O-CDs@ hydrogel (d).
The scheme illustration of synthesis of O-CDs and their applications.

head.ce:title: High-efficient and pH-sensitive orange luminescence from silicon-doped carbon dots for information encryption and bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: HKUST-Shenzhen Research Institute, No. 9 Yuexing first RD, Hi-tech Park, Nanshan, Shenzhen 518057, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploration of carbon dots (CDs) with high quantum yield, facile synthesis path and satisfying output for their multiple applications remains a challenge. Thus, a silicon-doped orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) is synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method o-phenylenediamine and ethyl orthosilicate as raw materials. The O-CDs exhibits a bright and non-excitation-dependent emission peaking at 580 nm, and the corresponding quantum yield could be greatly boosted from 39.2 % to 64.1 % by silicon doping. The obtained O-CDs possess good biocompatibility and promising luminescence stability with varying solvents, ionic concentrations and temperatures. Its bio-imaging ability is performed by incubating zebrafish embryos with O-CDs aqueous solution, and clear in-vivo fluorescent images are obtained. Furthermore, due to its high-efficient and specific pH-sensitive emission with excellent dispersibility, the O-CDs can be used as a fluorescent ink for dual-model data encry/decryption in both hand-writing and stamp printing. Therefore, the as-prepared O-CDs show the potential as promising candidate for biomedical diagnosis, data encryption, and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in various fields including white LED, display, biological field, fluorescent ink and anti-counterfeiting [1-8], due to its unique properties like excellent luminescent properties, low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Most of reported carbon dots exhibit efficient emission and are located in blue and green region [9-11]. But the autofluorescence of tissue hinder them from bio-imaging and sensing application. Long-wavelength-emitting (yellow/red/infrared region) carbon dots have advantages of deep penetration and low radiation damage, and long-wavelength emission is necessary primary color to achieve multicolor patterning. However, presently reported long-wavelength-emitting CDs generally suffer from the low quantum yield (QY) [12-14]. Therefore, the exploration of efficient long-wavelength-emitting CDs is a major challenge in further CDs development.

Using optical anti-counterfeiting ink is one of the most widely adopted security methods in bank notes, valuable merchandise and important documents, due to its advantages of easy-to-authenticate and hard-to-copy. The commonly used fluorescent materials, such as perovskite nanocrystals, metal organic frameworks and semiconductor quantum dots, are of high cost, toxicity and poor photostability [13,15]. Hence, anti-counterfeiting with rich information encryption by cheap, eco-friendly and stable fluorescent material is desired. Meanwhile, fluorescent imaging technique has unique advantages on monitoring intracellular species due to its high sensitivity, real-time imaging, and minimal damaging effect. An ideal bioimaging-oriented fluorescent material should enter cells and organisms without recycling, while exhibits long-wavelength emission with low cost and nontoxic [12]. Therefore, it is important and urgent to develop nanomaterials that can be used for both anticounterfeiting and cell imaging.

In this work, silicon-doped orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) are synthesized by a simple and economical one-pot hydrothermal method, using o-phenylenediamine and ethyl orthosilicate as raw materials (Scheme 1 ). Under 410 nm excitation, O-CDs shows broadband orange emissions peaking at ∼580 nm, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ∼110 nm and a high quantum yield of 64.1 %. O-CDs has good stability at various solvents, ionic concentrations and temperatures. Utilizing the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of O-CDs, a clear and safe fluorescent imaging of zebrafish embryos could be realized. Moreover, O-CDs show significant luminescent response to different pH value due to protonation and deprotonation process of its surface functional groups. It could be also utilized as the fluorescence ink for hand-writing and stamp printing, where the information encryption and decryption could be easily achieved by luminescence quenching and recovery using acid/base treatment. Furthermore, O-CDs can be solidified as starch fluorescent hydrogels, showing bright fluorescence under 395 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp.

The structure, composition and surface functional groups of silicon-doped O-CDs can be characterized by TEM, FT-IR and XPS. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the O-CDs are uniformly dispersed nano particles. The internal image (Fig. 1a inset) showed that the particle size distribution of O-CDs is ranged from 3 nm to 6 nm, and the average particle size is mainly around 4.8 nm. The surface functional groups of O-CDs are further analyzed by FT-IR (Fig. 1b). The stretching vibration of NH is located at 3378 cm−1. The -CC-H bonds were formed at 3182 cm−1 and the peak value is 1624 cm−1, which is derived from the Ar (CH) bond. The peak at 1498 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of = CH. The peak values near 1266 cm−1 indicate the presence of unsaturated CH bonds in the sample. Si-O-Si stretching vibrations peak are located at 1092 cm−1 in Si-doped O-CDs’ FT-IR, which is absence in that of neat O-CDs. The CN key position is 743 cm−1. Wide-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 1c) showed that Si (2P3), C (1 s), N (1 s) and O (1 s) had four main binding energy peaks at 130.9, 268.9, 399.4 and 533.4 eV, respectively. The contents of C, O, N and Si are 86.01%, 10.59 %, 2.13 % and 1.57 %, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 1d that C (1 s) peak can be decomposed into two sub-peaks at 286.8 eV (CO) and 292.8 eV (c-CO3). The typical peak at 400.8 and 402.2 eV in Fig. 1e belongs to the combination of amino N and N- (H)4. In addition, NO binding is verified by the binding peak at 533.3 eV of the high-resolution O (1 s) XPS spectrum (Fig. 1f). In Fig. 1g, the binding peak at 104.2 eV belongs to Si-O2, and the binding peak at 101.2 eV belongs to Si-C. Na and Cl peaks were observed in XPS spectra since NaCl was used to adsorb CDs in XPS analysis. These results indicate that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, which ensures its excellent dispersibility in polar solvents.

CDs exhibit outstanding solubility and strongest emission in ethanol and form a clear yellow solution. Thus, its absorption and emission spectra properties in ethanol solution are tested and represented in Fig. 2 a. A series of absorption bands located in UV and blue region are shown in PLE spectrum. Upon 410 nm excitation, O-CDs shows broadband orange emissions peaking at ∼ 580 nm with a full width at half maxima (FWHM) of ∼ 110 nm. A dramatic increase in the total emission intensity is observed with Si4+ doping in O-CDs. The total integrated intensity increases 1.64 times for Si4+-doped O-CDs in comparison with neat O-CDs. Quantum yield (QY) is an important parameter to evaluate the luminescent performance. A significant improvement on the QY with Si4+ doping could be observed, i.e. 39.2 % for neat O-CDs and 64.1 % for Si4+-doped O-CDs at the same concentration.

It is found that the use of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) as a coordinating and passivation agent can improve the QY of CDs. We believe that there are three possible reasons responsible for the PL enhancement. First, the introduction of silicon can produce a new surface state (the so-called Si-state) that traps electrons, increases radiative recombination, and suppresses nonradiative recombination. Second, silicon dopant as electron donors could increase the electron cloud density, thus increasing the probability of electron-hole radiative recombination. Third, TEOS functional groups can act as surface passivation agents, which greatly prevents vibration and rotation and aggregation of CDs, then eventually reduces the nonradiative process [16,17].

In order to verify the above-mentioned reasons, temperature-dependent PL of both neat and Si-doped O-CDs were measured (Fig. 2e and Fig. S1). The neat O-CDs keeps 84 % of its maximum at 65 °C, which is much lower than Si-doped O-CDs (98 % of its maximum at 65 °C). Therefore, the Si-doped O-CDs exhibit a better emission thermal stability, which preliminary proved that the silicon doping could decrease the vibration and rotation of PL center in the O-CDs that aggravated by high temperature. Generally, a fluorophore is excited by a photon and then the excited electron will drop to the ground state with a certain probability. The probability is based on the decay rates through radiative and nonradiative decay pathways.
τ =
1
k F
+
k
NF
; Q E =
k F
k F
+
k
NF

τ is the fluorescence lifetime, QE is the quantum efficiency; k F is the rate for radiative decay, k NF is the rate for nonradiative decay. The decay lifetimes and QE data of O-CDs synthesized with different TEOS volume are measured. The corresponding radiative and nonradiative rates are calculated and listed in Table. S1. It is found that k NF value shows an obvious decline after Si-doping.

In addition, O-CDs emission is found to be non-excitation dependent (Fig. 2b), i.e. the peak wavelength of O-CDs emission remains at 580 nm when the excitation wavelength gradually shifts from 370 nm to 450 nm. This verifies that there is one kind of emitting center in O-CDs. In reported literatures which o-phenylenediamine is one of raw materials, the BN-CDs have a maximum emission wavelength at 304 nm when excited at 277 nm, and the QY is as high as 31.5 % [18]. Moreover, N-CDs have the strongest emission peak appears at 555 nm excited at 440 nm and the QY is up to 12.6% [19]. Y-CDs with a quantum yield of 31% exhibited a emission peak at 570 nm with excitation at 450 nm [20]. Compared with them, O-CDs have longer-wavelength-emission and higher quantum yield.

The luminescent performance of CDs in different environments is particularly important for its multiple applications [21-23]. First, the emission variation of O-CDs in different solvent is shown in Fig. 2c. In the environment of isopropyl alcohol, acetone and ethanol with weak polarity, the emission intensity reaches its maximum with a slightly blue-shifted peak wavelength. Meanwhile, an obvious decrease of O-CDs emission intensity is found in the distilled water. The emission variation with the solvent polarity is probably attributed to the electron rearrangement on the surface of O-CDs with the increasing solvent polarity, resulting in a great change of the dipole moment. Meanwhile, both emission peak and intensity remains unchanged at various ion environments, which indicates a good ion-resistance of the O-CDs emission and is beneficial to maintain its luminescence when applied in a complex environment. The emission thermal stability of O-CDs is measured and shown in Fig. 2e. It is found that the strongest emission intensity is observed at 45 °C, which keeps 98 % and 88 % of the optimal fluorescence at 65 °C and 15 °C, respectively. Hence, the O-CDs emit a bright orange fluorescence with high quantum yield and no excitation dependence, while exhibits a good stability at various temperatures and ionic strength. The fluorescence decay curve of O-CDs is shown in Fig. 2g and close to bi-exponential. The average lifetime (τave) is calculated to be 1.96 ns. The fitting results (τ1 = 1.60 ns and τ2 = 20.24 ns) are in line with the typical carbon dots and corresponding composite materials.

The O-CDs is applied in transparent zebrafish embryos for in-vivo bioimaging. Through previous experiments, it is found that the fluorescence of O-CDs in zebrafish embryos reached the maximum after incubating for 12 h, and the differentiated parts such as eyes and tail could be clearly seen at 36 h after fertilization. Therefore, zebrafish embryos fertilized for 36 h are used in this experiment and co-incubated with different concentrations of O-CDs for 12 h for observation. As seen in the Fig. 3 , there is no fluorescence in the control group at 488 nm excitation. However, zebrafish embryos incubated with O-CDs for 12 h show strong orange fluorescence and uniform distribution of O-CDs could be seen in the fish body, suggesting that O-CDs could penetrate the embryo through the chorionic membrane. Moreover, with the increase of O-CDs concentration, the fluorescence gradually increases and reaches the maximum at optimal concentration of 4.00 mg/mL. However, when O-CDs concentration is beyond 4.00 mg/mL, zebrafish embryos died due to great changes in osmotic pressure at high O-CDs concentration. At optimal concentration, the O-CDs shows low toxicity and would not affect the activity abilities of zebrafish embryos, and thus can serve as a bio-safe and efficient reagent for vitro and vivo fluorescent-imaging application.

CDs show great response to different pH value so that it can be designed to be anti-counterfeiting ink. As seen in the Fig. 4 a, when the pH value is 7, the O-CDs show the strongest emission intensity. An obvious quench of emission intensity without peak wavelength shift is shown in either acidic (pH = 1 ∼ 7) and alkaline (pH = 7 ∼ 12) environment. The O-CDs solution with pH = 1 exhibits the weakest luminescence. In the process of increasing the pH values, no peak shift is found under the same excitation in emission recovery process, which could be attributed to the hydrolysis of surface functional groups of O-CDs in a strong acid-base environment, resulting in the change of fluorescence intensity. In order to probe the pH-sensitive luminescent mechanism, Fig. 4b shows luminescent decay curves of O-CDs before and after HCl addition, followed by a further NaOH spray. The lifetime signal can be detected in the original CD solution, and vanished after HCl addition. After further spraying NaOH, the lifetime signal is almost fully recovered in O-CDs. Such erasable/recovered fluorescence signal provides useful tool for the data encryption, which is due to protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on CDs surface in acidic and alkaline media. As seen in Fig. S2, there is no obvious change in FT-IR spectra of CDs under different pH environment. To explore further, zeta potential measurement is applied. According to Fig. S3, O-CDs is positively charged in either acidic (pH = 1 ∼ 5) and surface functional groups are protonated. The ζ potential of O-CDs reduces continuously with the increase of the pH and turns to negtive when pH rise to 5.65, implying that surface functional groups like amino and hydroxyl tend to be hydrolyzed at high pH.

With HCl addition, the protonation of CDs surface functional groups like the amino generates luminescent quencher sites, thus resulting in the emission intensity decrease. After spraying NaOH, deprotonation of the amino groups occurs on CD surface, which results in the enhanced radiative transitions and the luminescent signal recovers. The decay lifetimes and QE data under different pH values are measured. The corresponding radiative and nonradiative rates are calculated and listed in Table S2, in which the radiative rate rises with increasing pH and reaches the maximum at pH = 8. Basing on the interesting pH-sensitive luminescent property, the O-CDs solution can be applied to advanced security inks for information encryption and decryption in both hand-writing and stamp printing. An easy, simple, and repeatable encryption/decryption model is developed using O-CDs pH-dependent orange emission under UV excitation. A poetry is hand-writing onto a non-fluorescent A4 paper with O-CDs solution as ink. As 1st step of encryption, the written information cannot be observed under sunlight illumination conditions (Fig. 4c). Then the information could be decrypted as orange fluorescent mark is observed under the 395 nm UV lamp. In 2nd encryption process, the HCl solution is sprayed onto the paper and the bright luminescence vanish on the paper. So the encrypted information cannot be observed either in daylight or UV irradiation. For 2nd decryption, NaOH solution is spayed on paper then dried in air. The orange luminescent hand-writing is recovered and seen again by the naked eye at near ultraviolet (NUV) irradiation. Furthermore, as can be seen in Fig. 5 , the stamp can clearly print the pattern and present a bright orange light under the ultraviolet lamp. This fluorescence can also be quenched by acid and restored by alkali. Thus, this O-CDs can be a potential candidate for anti-counterfeiting and data encryption.

Finally, in order to explore whether O-CDs can be solidified, O-CDs aqueous solution is simply blended with starch, then the solvent is evaporated at 80 ℃, and O-CDs starch powder is obtained after evenly grinding. As shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b, starch powder of O-CDs is light yellow under daylight and emits bright yellow-green fluorescence under 395 nm UV lamp through 450 nm filter. As shown in Fig. 6c, the emission wavelength is shifted from the original 580 nm blue to 545 nm. However, when the agar powder is used as the composite material, O-CDs is distributed on the hydrogel and showed orange-yellow fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp at 395 nm. As can be seen in Fig. 6d, the emission wavelength is shifted from the original 580 nm blue to 550 nm. The results show that O-CDs can be dispersed into different hosts to realize the solidification without luminescent quenching.

In summary, silicon-doped orange-emitting carbon dot (O-CDs) with high quantum yield are prepared by using o-phenylenediamine and ethyl orthosilicate as raw materials through a convenient hydrothermal method. At 410 nm excitation, O-CDs shows broadband orange emissions peaking at ∼580 nm with FWHM of ∼110 nm. Emission improvements of O-CDs could be achieved by Si doping, leading to the increased QE from 39.2 % to 64.1% with excellent fluorescence stability. Silicon doping could result in improvement in quantum yield (39.2 % and 64.1 % for neat- and Si-doped O-CDs) and enhanced emission thermal stability (84 % and 98 % of maximum at 65 °C for neat- and Si-doped O-CDs). This O-CDs owns a high quantum efficiency compared with reported CDs in which o-phenylenediamine is used as raw materials [18-20,24].It is worth noting that O-CDs exhibit low toxicity and good biocompatibility, and could provide clear & safe florescent imaging of zebrafish embryos. Furthermore, the pH-dependent erasable/recovered phosphorescence signal of O-CDs, which is due to protonation and deprotonation process of CDs surface functional groups, makes it suitable as anti-counterfeiting ink for both hand-writing and stamp printing. The desired information and pattern can be easily encrypted and decrypted through UV irradiation and acid/alkali treatment. A successful solidification of O-CDs is realized by blended it with starch and AGAR powder, where bright orange emission remains in both phosphor and hydrogel. All results suggest that O-CDs have great potential in biomedical diagnosis, data encryption, and anti-counterfeiting
Zishan Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Weiying Zhou: . Jiabao Luo: . Jiaqi Fan: . Zhan-chao Wu: . Hongni Zhu: . Jinqing Huang: . Xinguo Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The research is funded by Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260),

National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081). Jinqing Huang acknowledges the financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (No. JCYJ20180306174930894).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data (Materials and instruments, characterization, preparation of the orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs), zebrafish maintenance and imaging, preparation of the anti-counterfeiting ink, and preparation of the starch and gel by O-CDs can be found in the supporting information. Fig. S1 depicts the emission intensity variation of neat-OCDs at various temperatures. Fig. S2 depicts the FT-IR spectera of O-CDs under different pH environments. Fig. S3 describes the Zeta potential of O-CDs under different pH environments. Table. S1 and S2 show the variation of lifetime, QE, radiative and nonradiative rate of O-CDs synthesized with different TEOS volume, and the variation of lifetime, QE, radiative and nonradiative rate of O-CDs under different pH environments, respectively.) to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.188.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
